Tshebesepe v S (A132/2020) [2020] ZAGPPHC 726 (11 December 2020)
Download original files
PDF format

IN THE HIGH COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA
GAUTENG DIVISION, PRETORIA
Case number: A132/2020
In the matter between:
N V TSHEBESEPE           
APPELLANT
versus
THE STATE                     
     RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
MAKHOBA
J
The
	appellant was convicted in the Regional Court of Pretoria on two
	counts of rape (contravention of section 3 of Act 32 of 2007)
and he
	was acquitted on the third count which is the count of kidnapping.
	For purposes of sentence both counts were taken as
one and the
	appellant was sentenced to life imprisonment. The appeal is against
	sentence only.
The
	basis of the appeal is that the court a quo erred in finding that
	there are no substantial and compelling circumstances justifying
	deviation from the prescribed minimum sentence.
The Counsel for the appellant submitted to us that the appellant was
	relatively young at the time of the commission of the offences,
a
	first-time offender and addicted to heroin.
It
	is further submitted on behalf of the appellant that this is not one
	of the worst cases of rape and that life imprisonment
is strictly
	disproportionate to the circumstances of the offences and ought to
	be set aside.
AD
SENTENCE
The appellant has been convicted of a serious offence. His
	conviction falls within the purview of Section 51 of Act 105 of 1997
	which prescribed minimum sentence, unless substantial and compelling
	circumstances were found to exist.
The
	appeal court must find that there are substantial and compelling
	circumstances justifying a lesser sentence or that the court
a
	quo
misdirected
	itself or did not exercise its judicial discretion properly and
	judicially, for the appeal to succeed.
In
S v Vilakazi
2009 (1)
	SACR 552
SCA at page 574 at para 58
it was held that in cases of serious crimes, the personal
	circumstances of the offender by themselves will necessarily recede
	to the background. The prescribed minimum sentences are not to be
	departed from lightly and for flimsy reasons:
S
	v Malgas
2001 (1) SACR 469
(SCA) (Approved by the constitutional
	Court in S v Dodo
2001 (2) SACR 594
(cc) at pages 602-603.
In S v C
1996 (2) SACR 181
at page 186 paragraph D-E.
“Rape is regarded by the society as one of the most heinous of
crimes and rightly so. A rapist does not murder his victim;
he
murders her self- respect and destroys her feeling of physical and
mental integrity and security. His monstrous deeds often
haunt his
victim and subject her to mental torment for the rest of her life. A
fate so often worst than loss of life”.
The basic approach in every appeal against sentence was set out in S
	v Rabie
1975 (4) SA 455
(A) at 857 D-F to be the following: the
	court hearing the appeal-
“(a) should be guided by the principles that punishment is
“pre-eminently a   matter for the discretion of
the
trial court”, and
(b) should be careful not to erode such discretion: hence the further
principle that the sentence should only be altered if the
discretion
has not been ‘judicially and properly exercised.”
The test under (b) is whether the sentence is validated by any
irregularity or      misdirection or is
disturbingly inappropriate ( see also S v Giannoulis
1975 (4) SA 869
(A), S v Barnard 2004 (1) SACR 191 (SCA) at 194 C-D, S v Mayisela
2013(2) SACR 129 (GNP) at [13].
The
	court in S v Malgas
2001 (1) SACR 469
(SCA) at 478E-H said the
	appeal court can only interfere with the sentence imposed by the
	trial court where it is vitiated by
a material misdirection or where
	the disparity between the sentence of the trial court and the
	sentence that the appellate court
would have imposed had it been the
	trial court, is so marked that it be described as ‘shocking’,
	startling 
or disturbingly inappropriate ( see also Madiba v S
[2015] JOL 33686
(SCA)).
The
	appellant when he raped the complainant for the first time he showed
	no signs of intoxication or being under the influence
of drugs hence
	he was able to go buy cigarette and proceeded to the shack where the
	complainant was and raped her for the second
time. According to the
	complainant the appellant was awake the entire night.
In
	my view the assertion by the appellant that he must have been
	influenced by the taking of drugs or alcohol is devoid of any
truth
	and must be rejected.
I can find no grounds to interfere with the sentence imposed by the
	court
a quo.
The sentence imposed by the court
a quo
does
	not appear to be shockingly and disproportionate thus inducing a
	sense of shock. I find that the court
a quo
exercised its
	discretion properly and judicially.
Thus, the appeal against sentence is devoid of any merit and stands
	to be dismissed.
In
	the premises, the following order is made:
15.1 The appeal against sentence is dismissed.
15.2 The conviction and sentence of the court
a quo
is
confirmed.
_________________________________
D MAKHOBA
JUDGE OF THE HIGH COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA,
GAUTENG DIVISION
I AGREE
_________________________________
W. HUGHES
JUDGE OF THE HIGH COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA,
GAUTENG
DIVISION
